title: Short Selling and Other Share Strategies
objectives: Explain how short selling works and its risks
Describe share buybacks and why companies do them
Identify strategies beyond simple buy-and-hold investing

vocabulary.definition: Borrowing shares from someone, selling them, then hoping to buy them back cheaper later. Profits when share prices fall. Risk: the price can rise instead, causing unlimited losses.
When a company uses its own cash to buy its own shares from the market, reducing the total shares on issue. Increases earnings per share for remaining shareholders.
Investing a fixed amount at regular intervals (e.g. $100 per month) regardless of price. Automatically buys more shares when cheap and fewer when expensive.
Borrowing money to invest in shares. Amplifies both gains and losses. Dangerous for most investors — if shares fall, losses can exceed the original investment.

Buying more shares in a company after the price has fallen, lowering the average cost per share. Can be a good strategy if the company is fundamentally sound, but can amplify losses if the company keeps falling.

activities.title: Short Selling — Profiting From Falls
activities.notePrompt: Write: How does short selling work? What is the maximum profit possible on a short? What is the maximum possible loss? Why is the loss potentially unlimited?
activities.content:
Most investors profit when share prices rise. Short sellers do the opposite — they profit when prices fall.

How short selling works:
1. You borrow 100 shares of Mainfreight from a broker (paying a fee).
2. You immediately sell those borrowed shares for $70 each = $7,000 cash.
3. Time passes. Mainfreight falls to $50.
4. You buy 100 shares at $50 = $5,000.
5. You return the 100 shares to the broker.
6. Profit: $7,000 received − $5,000 spent = $2,000 (minus borrowing fees).

The risks of short selling:
— Maximum profit: 100% of the amount borrowed-and-sold (if the company goes to $0)
— Maximum loss: unlimited. If Mainfreight rises to $140 instead of falling, you must buy back at $140: $7,000 − $14,000 = −$7,000 loss. If it rises to $200, loss = $13,000. There is no ceiling on losses.

Short selling by experienced hedge funds serves a function: it provides price-discovery and exposes overvalued companies. It is not appropriate for most ordinary investors.

NZ short selling is legal and disclosed — significant short positions in NZX shares must be reported to the FMA.

activities.title: Share Buybacks — Companies Buying Their Own Shares
activities.notePrompt: Write: If a company has 100 million shares on issue, earns $50 million profit, and buys back 10 million shares — what happens to earnings per share?
activities.content:
When a company generates more cash than it needs for operations and growth, it can return that cash to shareholders in two ways: pay a dividend, or buy back its own shares.

How a buyback works: the company announces it will spend (e.g.) $50 million buying its own shares on the NZX over the next 12 months. It becomes a buyer in the market. The purchased shares are usually cancelled, reducing the total shares on issue.

Effect on earnings per share:
Before buyback: Company earns $50 million profit on 100 million shares = $0.50 EPS.
After buying back 10 million shares: $50 million profit ÷ 90 million shares = $0.556 EPS — a 11.1% increase in EPS with no improvement in underlying profits.

Why companies do buybacks:
— Returns cash to shareholders in a tax-effective way (capital gain vs dividend income tax treatment)
— Signals management believes the shares are undervalued
— Improves EPS and therefore potentially the share price

Buybacks vs dividends: Some investors prefer buybacks (NZ imputation credits make dividends relatively efficient too, reducing the tax advantage of buybacks vs other markets). Companies like Apple and Mainfreight have used buybacks significantly.

activities.statement: Short selling a share carries a maximum possible loss of 100% — the same as buying a share.
activities.explanation: False. When you buy a share, the maximum you can lose is 100% of your investment (if the share goes to zero). When you short sell, your maximum loss is theoretically unlimited — because the share price can rise without limit. If you short sell 100 shares at $70 ($7,000) and the price rises to $300, you must buy back at $300 × 100 = $30,000 — a loss of $23,000 on a $7,000 short position. This asymmetric risk profile makes short selling significantly more dangerous than buying shares for most investors.

activities.title: Dollar-Cost Averaging — The Simple Strategy That Works
activities.notePrompt: Tama invests $200 per month in the NZX 50 index fund at these prices: Jan $12, Feb $10, Mar $8, Apr $10, May $12. Calculate the total units purchased. Calculate the average cost per unit.
activities.content:
Dollar-cost averaging (DCA) is the strategy of investing a fixed amount at regular intervals regardless of price. It is one of the most reliable long-term strategies available to ordinary investors.

How it works with $200 monthly in a NZX 50 fund:
January (price $12.00): $200 ÷ $12 = 16.67 units
February (price $10.00): $200 ÷ $10 = 20.00 units
March (price $8.00): $200 ÷ $8 = 25.00 units
April (price $10.00): $200 ÷ $10 = 20.00 units
May (price $12.00): $200 ÷ $12 = 16.67 units

Total invested: $1,000
Total units: 98.34
Average cost per unit: $1,000 ÷ 98.34 = $10.17

Average price over the period: ($12+$10+$8+$10+$12) ÷ 5 = $10.40 per unit

DCA average cost ($10.17) is LOWER than the simple average price ($10.40) — because you automatically bought more units when the price was low (25 units in March) and fewer when high (16.67 units in January and May). You did not need to time the market at all.

DCA is perfect for KiwiSaver (automatic fortnightly contributions) and for young investors building wealth steadily over time.

questions.question: You short sell 200 Spark shares at $4.20. Spark rises to $6.80 instead of falling. What is your loss?
options: $0 — your loss is capped at the original $840 short sale value
$520 loss — you profit when prices fall so a rise causes a proportional loss of 200 × $2.60
$840 loss — you lose the full amount of the original short sale
$840 gain — short sellers profit from any price movement
questions.explanation: Short sale proceeds: 200 × $4.20 = $840. Buyback cost: 200 × $6.80 = $1,360. Loss: $1,360 − $840 = $520. You sold borrowed shares for $840 and now must pay $1,360 to replace them — a $520 loss. And importantly, if Spark kept rising to $10, the loss would be 200 × ($10 − $4.20) = $1,160. Short selling losses are not capped and grow with every price increase.

questions.question: A company earns $80 million and has 200 million shares on issue ($0.40 EPS). It buys back 40 million shares. What is the new EPS?
options: $0.40 — EPS is unchanged because profits did not increase
$0.32 — EPS falls because the company spent money on the buyback
$0.50 — same $80M profit now divided by 160M remaining shares
$0.80 — EPS doubles because half the shares are cancelled
questions.explanation: $80 million profit ÷ (200M − 40M) = $80M ÷ 160M shares = $0.50 EPS. The buyback reduced shares from 200M to 160M. Same profit, fewer shares — each remaining share represents a larger portion of the company's earnings. EPS rises 25% ($0.40 to $0.50) without any improvement in the underlying business — which is why buybacks are popular and tend to be positively received by the market.

activities.title: Match the Strategy to Its Mechanism

pairs.item: Investing $150 per month regardless of what the market is doing
Borrowing shares, selling them, then buying back cheaper after a fall
Company uses excess cash to reduce shares on issue
Buying more of a fallen share to reduce your average cost
Borrowing money from your broker to invest more than you have in cash

activities.explanation: These strategies range from conservative (DCA) to highly risky (short selling, margin lending). For young investors, DCA into diversified index funds is widely regarded as the most appropriate strategy. Short selling and margin lending amplify risk in ways that can result in losses exceeding the original investment.

activities.statement: Dollar-cost averaging always produces a lower average cost per unit than investing a lump sum at the start of the period.
activities.explanation: False. If prices rise consistently throughout the investment period, a lump sum invested at the start would outperform DCA (because you bought all units at the lower starting price). DCA produces a lower average cost than the simple average price over the period, but not necessarily lower than the starting price. DCA's main advantage is psychological and practical — it removes the need to time the market, eliminates the risk of investing a lump sum at a peak, and is suitable for investors who invest income gradually rather than having a large sum at the start.

activities.question: Zoe invests $300 every month in the NZX 50. In March the price is $10.50. How many units does she buy in March?
activities.placeholder: Type the number of units here...
activities.hint: Units = amount invested ÷ price per unit.
activities.explanation: $300 ÷ $10.50 = 28.571 units. Dollar-cost averaging produces fractional unit purchases like this — which is why platforms like Sharesies that allow fractional unit investing are particularly well-suited to DCA strategies. Most index fund platforms automatically reinvest contributions and allow partial unit purchases.

activities.title: Which Strategy For You?
activities.prompt: Of the strategies covered today — buy-and-hold, dollar-cost averaging, short selling, share buybacks (from company perspective), averaging down — which would you use personally and why? Which would you definitely avoid and why? Write three to four sentences justifying your choices based on your investment goals and risk tolerance.
activities.nceaLink: Extra Learning — matching your investment strategy to your personal situation is a core financial literacy skill.
